thesis.text: Selecting text in the xterm terminal now copies it to the clipboard automatically and shows a "Text copied" toast, and the existing keyboard copy shortcut is rerouted through the same deduplicated copy helper.

evidence.name: Copy helper with toast and dedup
Selection-change listener drives the copy

reason.text: The entire behavior change lives here: a per-terminal lastCopiedSelection, the copyTerminalSelection helper, the onSelectionChange listener, and the keyboard copy path rerouted through the shared helper.

evidence.name: Keyboard copy reuses the helper

reason.text: New test file: 190 lines of mock scaffolding for xterm, Tauri plugins, and the terminal lib, then four tests that prove listener wiring, the toast payload, the empty-selection guard, and dedup.

evidence.name: Copy and toast proven through the mocked terminal

claim.text: Any non-empty terminal selection is written to the clipboard and answered with a closable "Text copied" success toast; the keyboard copy shortcut now shows the same toast because it calls the same helper.

evidence.name: Toast on successful clipboard write

claim.text: lastCopiedSelection is per-terminal-instance effect state that suppresses copying the identical selection twice; it resets when the selection is cleared, so reselecting the same text copies and toasts again.

evidence.name: Dedup state declared beside other effect-local state
Reset on cleared selection

line_map.title: Drag events can copy intermediate selections

claim.text: The PR description says selections are copied "after pointer release", but the code copies on every onSelectionChange event with no pointer gating. xterm emits selection changes while a drag extends the selection, so each intermediate selection ("h", "he", "hel", ...) can trigger its own clipboard write and toast; the dedup only stops exact repeats, not growing prefixes.

evidence.name: Copy driven directly by selection change, no pointer-release gate

claim.text: A failed clipboard write is swallowed by an empty catch, and lastCopiedSelection is set before the write resolves, so after a failure the same selection is treated as already copied: no error surfaced, and retrying via the keyboard shortcut is a silent no-op until the selection is cleared.

evidence.name: Dedup recorded before the async write, failure swallowed

line_map.title: Unit coverage misses drag and failure paths

claim.text: Four unit tests prove the listener wiring, the toast payload, the empty-selection guard, and the exact-repeat dedup through a hand-written xterm mock. They fire selection changes one at a time, so they do not prove drag-time behavior or the failure path.

evidence.name: Dedup proven for an exact repeat

line_map.title: Terminal mock duplicates the module surface

claim.text: The test file re-implements the terminal's entire ./lib surface as a mock, so any signature change in that module breaks this test file even when select-to-copy behavior is unaffected.

evidence.name: Full ./lib module mocked by hand

risks.title: Toast and clipboard spam while dragging a selection

claim.text: Because copying is driven by onSelectionChange with no pointer-release gating, dragging a selection can fire a clipboard write plus a success toast for every intermediate selection. The dedup key only blocks identical strings, so growing selections all pass. Needs a manual drag check; a pointerup/mouseup gate or debounce would match the PR's stated design.

evidence.name: No pointer gating on the copy trigger

risks.title: Failed copy is silent and blocks retry of the same selection

claim.text: lastCopiedSelection is recorded before writeClipboardText resolves and the catch is empty, so a failed write shows nothing and makes the keyboard shortcut a no-op for that selection. Resetting lastCopiedSelection in the catch would restore retry.

evidence.name: Dedup set before the write, empty catch

risks.title: Test scaffolding duplicates the ./lib module surface

claim.text: The hand-written mock of every ./lib export couples this test file to the module's full API; extracting the copy logic into a testable unit would shrink the mock and the breakage surface.

evidence.name: Hand-maintained module mock

claim.text: A selection change on the mocked terminal writes the selected text to the clipboard and creates the exact toast payload; CI's Frontend, Rust, and smoke jobs all passed on the head commit.

evidence.name: Wiring test

claim.text: Firing the same selection twice produces exactly one clipboard write and one toast.

evidence.name: Dedup assertions

verification.title: Drag-time behavior in a real terminal

claim.text: The mock fires selection changes only when a test calls fireSelectionChange manually, so nothing proves how many copies and toasts a real mouse drag produces, which is exactly where the pointer-release claim in the PR description matters.

evidence.name: Selection changes are test-driven, not drag-driven

questions.title: Verify real drag behavior
questions.text: Was a real mouse drag verified manually? The description says copy happens "after pointer release", but the code copies on each onSelectionChange, and xterm can emit those while the drag is still extending the selection.

evidence.name: Selection listener without pointer gating

summary.text: The PR is already merged and the happy path is small, well-factored, and unit-tested: one helper owns copy, dedup, and toast for both the mouse and keyboard paths. The open items are behavioral, not structural: confirm drag selection does not spam toasts, and make a failed copy retryable.

evidence.name: Single helper shared by both copy paths

follow_ups.text: Gate the copy on pointer release (or debounce selection changes) so a drag produces one copy and one toast, matching the PR's stated design.

evidence.name: Current trigger fires per selection change

follow_ups.text: Reset lastCopiedSelection when writeClipboardText rejects so the user can retry the same selection, and consider surfacing the failure instead of an empty catch.

evidence.name: Failure path leaves dedup set
